quality.rationale: 多步推导结合局部坐标系构造、测地线方程与黎曼张量展开，逻辑严密且具实际物理背景

origin.question:
[文件 1: 1_s14_14.12.md]

Problem 14.12. An observer in a closed box (not necessarily in free fall) measures positions and times inside his box with rulers and a clock. Show that the equation of motion for a particle, correct to first order in its measured velocity $v << 1$ and position $x^j$ is
$$
\begin{array}{l} \mathrm {d} \mathbf {v}^{\mathrm {j}} / \mathrm {d} t = 2 (\underline {\mathbf {v}} \times \underline {\omega})^{\mathrm {j}} - [ (\underline {\mathbf {x}} \times \underline {\omega}) \times \underline {\omega} ]^{\mathrm {j}} + (\underline {\mathbf {x}} \times \underline {\mathrm {d}} \underline {\omega} / \mathrm {d} t)^{\mathrm {j}} \\\\ - \mathrm {a}^{\mathrm {j}} (1 + \underline {\underline {\mathrm {a}}} \cdot \underline {\underline {\mathrm {x}}}) - \mathrm {R}_{0 \mathrm {k 0}}^{\mathrm {j}} \mathrm {x}^{\mathrm {k}}. \\\\ \end{array}
$$
Here $\underline{\omega}$ is the angular velocity of the box, $\underline{\mathbf{a}}$ its acceleration, and $\mathbb{R}^j_{0k0}$ the Riemann tensor evaluated at the origin.

## Solution
origin.solution:
Solution 14.12. In geometrical language the observer sets up his local coordinates $\mathbf{x}^{\alpha}$ as follows: If $\mathbf{u}$ is the 4-velocity of the center of his box along its worldline, he chooses $\mathbf{e}_0 = \mathbf{u}$ and $\mathbf{e}_{\alpha} \cdot \mathbf{e}_{ eta} = \eta_{\alpha  eta}$ along his world line. From each point $\mathbf{P}(	au)$ on his world-line ( $	au =$ proper time), he sends out spatial geodesics orthogonal to $\mathbf{u}$ , with affine parameter equal to proper length $\mathbf{s}$ . If $\mathbf{n}$ is the tangent vector to the spatial geodesic through the point $\mathbf{P}$ near his world-line, then he assigns to $\mathbf{P}$ the coordinates $\mathbf{x}^0 = 	au$ , $\mathbf{x}^{\mathbf{k}} = \mathbf{sn}^{\mathbf{k}}$ .
The equation of motion is now
$$
\frac {\mathrm {d}^{2} \mathbf {x}^{\alpha}}{\mathrm {d}\lambda^{2}} + \Gamma^{\alpha}_{\beta\gamma} \frac {\mathrm {d} \mathbf {x}^{\beta}}{\mathrm {d}\lambda} \frac {\mathrm {d} \mathbf {x}^{\gamma}}{\mathrm {d}\lambda} = 0 \tag {1}
$$
where we can take $\lambda$ to be the proper time of the particle. The 4-velocity of the particle is $\mathrm{dx}^{\alpha} / \mathrm{d}\lambda = (\gamma, \gamma \underline{\mathbf{v}})$ , where $\gamma = (1 - \mathbf{v}^{2})^{-\frac{1}{2}}$ . First change $\mathrm{d} / \mathrm{d}\lambda$ to $\mathrm{d} / \mathrm{d}t$ in Equation (1) using $\mathrm{d} / \mathrm{d}\lambda = \gamma \mathrm{d} / \mathrm{d}t$ :
$$
\frac {\mathrm {d}^{2} \mathbf {x}^{\alpha}}{\mathrm {d}t^{2}} + \frac {1}{\gamma} \frac {\mathrm {d}\gamma}{\mathrm {d}t} \frac {\mathrm {d}x^{\alpha}}{\mathrm {d}t} + \Gamma^{\alpha}_{ eta\gamma} \frac {\mathrm {d} \mathbf {x}^{\beta}}{\mathrm {d}t} \frac {\mathrm {d} \mathbf {x}^{\gamma}}{\mathrm {d}t} = 0. \tag {2}
$$
Putting $a = 0$ , we get
$$
\frac {1}{\gamma} \frac {\mathrm {d}\gamma}{\mathrm {d}t} + \Gamma^{0}_{ eta\gamma} \frac {\mathrm {d}\mathbf {x}^{ eta}}{\mathrm {d}t} \frac {\mathrm {d}\mathbf {x}^{\gamma}}{\mathrm {d}t} = 0.
$$
Substitute this in Equation (2) and get, for $\alpha = j$
$$
\frac {\mathrm {d} \mathbf {v}^{\mathrm {j}}}{\mathrm {d}t} + (- \mathbf {v}^{\mathrm {j}} \Gamma^{0}_{ eta\gamma} + \Gamma^{\mathrm {j}}_{ eta\gamma}) \frac {\mathrm {d}\mathbf {x}^{ eta}}{\mathrm {d}t} \frac {\mathrm {d}\mathbf {x}^{\gamma}}{\mathrm {d}t} = 0. \tag {3}
$$
To first order in $\mathbf{v}$ , Equation (3) is
$$
\frac {\mathrm {d} \mathbf {v}^{\mathrm {j}}}{\mathrm {d}t} - \mathbf {v}^{\mathrm {j}} \Gamma_{00}^{0} + \Gamma_{00}^{\mathrm {j}} + 2 \Gamma_{\mathrm {k}0}^{\mathrm {j}} \mathbf {v}^{\mathrm {k}} = 0. \tag {4}
$$
Thus, to first order in $\underline{\mathbf{x}}$ and $\underline{\mathbf{y}}$ , we have
$$
\frac {\mathrm {d} \mathrm {v}^{\mathrm {j}}}{\mathrm {d}t} = \left. \mathrm {v}^{\mathrm {j}} \Gamma_{00}^{\mid}ight|_{\underset {\mathrm {x}=0}{\mid}} - \left. \Gamma_{00}^{\mathrm {j}}ight|_{\underset {\mathrm {x}=0}{\mid}} - \left. \mathrm {x}^{\mathrm {k}} \Gamma_{00,\mathrm {k}}^{\mathrm {j}}ight|_{\underset {\mathrm {x}=0}{\mid}} - 2 \mathrm {v}^{\mathrm {k}} \Gamma_{\mathrm {k}0}^{\mathrm {j}} |_{\underset {\mathrm {x}=0}{\mid}}. \tag {5}
$$
The Christoffel symbols at $\underline{\mathbf{x}} = 0$ are found using a result of Problem 7.17:
$$
\Gamma_{\alpha0}^{ eta} \mathbf {e}_{ eta} = 
abla_{\mathbf {u}} \mathbf {e}_{\alpha} = - \Omega_{\alpha}^{ eta} \mathbf {e}_{ eta}
$$
$$
\Gamma_{ eta\alpha0} = - \Omega_{ eta\alpha} \equiv a_{\alpha} u_{ eta} - a_{ eta} u_{\alpha} + \varepsilon_{\alpha eta\lambda\sigma} u^{\lambda} \omega^{\sigma}.
$$
Since $\mathbf{u}^{\alpha} = (1,\underline{0}),\mathbf{u}_{\alpha} = (-1,\underline{0}),\mathbf{a}_{\alpha} = (0,\underline{\mathbf{a}}), oldsymbol{\omega}^{\sigma} = (0,\underline{ oldsymbol{\omega}})$ we get
$$
\Gamma_{ eta a 0} = 0 \quad 	ext {i f} \quad  eta = a
$$
$$
\Gamma_{0 j 0} = - \Gamma_{j 0 0} = - a_{j}
$$
$$
\Gamma_{\mathrm {k j}0} = - \Gamma_{\mathrm {j k}0} = \varepsilon_{\mathrm {j k}0\mathrm {m}} \omega^{\mathrm {m}} = \varepsilon_{\mathrm {j k m}} \omega^{\mathrm {m}} \tag {6}
$$
The $\Gamma$ 's with no 0-index are found from the condition that the coordinate lines $x^{\alpha} = (r, \mathfrak{sn}^{\mathbf{k}})$ , where $r$ and $\mathfrak{n}^{\mathbf{k}}$ are independent of $\mathbf{s}$ , are geodesics. Thus,
$$
\begin{array}{l} 0 = \frac {\mathrm {d}^{2} \mathbf {x}^{\alpha}}{\mathrm {d}s^{2}} + \Gamma^{\alpha}  eta\gamma \frac {\mathrm {d}\mathbf {x}^{ eta}}{\mathrm {d}s} \frac {\mathrm {d}\mathbf {x}^{\gamma}}{\mathrm {d}s} \\ = 0 + \Gamma_{j k}^{\alpha} n^{j} n^{k}. \\\\ \end{array}
$$
This is satisfied at $\underline{\mathbf{x}} = 0$ for arbitrary $\underline{\mathbf{n}}$ , so
$$
\Gamma_{\alpha\mathrm {i k}} = 0. \tag {7}
$$
We find $\Gamma_{00,\mathbf {k}}^{\mathbf {j}}$ from the expression for the Riemann tensor:
$$
\mathsf {R}_{ eta\gamma\delta}^{\alpha} = \Gamma_{ eta\delta ,\gamma}^{\alpha} - \Gamma_{ eta\gamma ,\delta}^{\alpha} + \Gamma_{\gamma\mu}^{\alpha} \Gamma_{ eta\delta}^{\mu} - \Gamma_{\delta\mu}^{\alpha} \Gamma_{ eta\gamma}^{\mu}.
$$
This gives
$$
\Gamma_{00,\mathbf {k}}^{\mathrm {j}} = \mathsf {R}_{0\mathbf {k}0}^{\mathrm {j}} + \Gamma_{0\mathbf {k},0}^{\mathrm {j}} - \Gamma_{\mathbf {k}\mu}^{\mathrm {j}} \Gamma_{00}^{\mu} + \Gamma_{0\mu}^{\mathrm {j}} \Gamma_{0\mathbf {k}}^{\mu}.
$$
At $\underline{\mathbf{x}} = 0$
$$
\Gamma_{0k,0}^{j} = - \varepsilon_{jk m} \omega^{m},
$$
$$
\Gamma_{\mathbf {k}\mu}^{\mathrm {j}} \Gamma_{00}^{\mu} = 0
$$
$$
\begin{array}{l} \Gamma_{0\mu}^{\mathrm {j}} \Gamma_{0\mathrm {k}}^{\mu} = \Gamma_{00}^{\mathrm {j}} \Gamma_{0\mathrm {k}}^{0} + \Gamma_{0\mathrm {m}}^{\mathrm {j}} \Gamma_{0\mathrm {k}}^{\mathrm {m}} \\ = a_{j} a_{k} + \varepsilon_{\min} \omega^{n} \varepsilon_{k m 1} \omega^{1}, \\\\ \end{array}
$$
and thus
$$
\begin{array}{l}  rac {\mathrm {d}\mathbf {v}^{\mathrm {j}}}{\mathrm {d}t} = - a_{\mathrm {j}} - \mathbf {x}^{\mathrm {k}} \left(\mathrm {R}_{0\mathrm {k}0}^{\mathrm {j}} - \varepsilon_{\mathrm {j k m}} \omega^{\mathrm {m}}_{,\mathrm {0}} + a_{\mathrm {j}} a_{\mathrm {k}} + \varepsilon_{\mathrm {m j n}} \omega^{\mathrm {n}} \varepsilon_{\mathrm {k m}1} \omega^{\mathrm {1}}ight) + 2 \varepsilon_{\mathrm {j k m}} \omega^{\mathrm {m}} \mathbf {v}^{\mathrm {k}} \\ = - a_{j} \left(1 + \underline {a} \cdot \underline {x}ight) - R^{j}_{0 k 0} x^{k} + \left(\underline {x} 	imes \underline {\omega},_{0}ight)^{j} - \left[ \left(\underline {x} 	imes \underline {\omega}ight) 	imes \underline {\omega}ight]^{j} + 2 \left(\underline {v} 	imes \underline {\omega}ight)^{j}. \\\\ \end{array}
$$
The first term is the “‘inertial force’” due to the acceleration of the reference frame. The $1 + \underline{a} \cdot \underline{x}$ is a relativistic correction factor (see MTW Exercise 37-4). The second term is the “‘true’” gravitational force. In the weak-field limit, a Newtonian physicist would separate $\underline{a}$ into a contribution from the local acceleration of gravity, $\partial \Phi / \partial x_{j}$ , plus an “absolute” acceleration $\underline{a}_{\mathrm{abs}}$ . Thus,
$$
\begin{array}{l} a_{j} + R_{0 k 0}^{j} x^{k} = \left(a_{j}ight)_{\mathrm {a b s}} + \partial \Phi / \partial x^{j} |_{\underline {x}=0} + \partial^{2} \Phi / \partial x^{j} \partial x^{k} |_{\underline {x}=0} x^{k} \\ = \left. \left(a_{j}ight)_{	ext {a b s}} + \partial \Phi / \partial x^{j}ight|_{\underset {\sim} {x}=x \underset {\sim} {p a r t i c l e}}. \\\\ \end{array}
$$
The terms involving $\omega$ are the same as in nonrelativistic mechanics; the second and third are the centrifugal and Coriolis forces respectively.
